html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: five sequential camera frustums spaced along the Z-axis to represent discrete positions of forward motion
active physical camera frustum at Z-coordinate with its semi-transparent projection plane
long red translation path vector arrow running through all camera centers along the Z-axis
target 3D scene: a vertical grid-patterned wall representing a planar surface
secondary target 3D scene: floating colored spheres at varying depths (Z-coordinates)
Focus of Expansion (FOE) marker (bright yellow dot) at the center of the active camera's projection plane
2D optical flow vector arrows drawn on the active camera's projection plane, pointing radially outward from the FOE
visual projection rays connecting 3D scene points through the camera lens center to the projection plane
ground plane with depth grid markings to emphasize the forward progression

interactions.label: Camera Position (Z)
interactions.teaches: Moving the camera closer along the Z-axis shows how the projected features expand rapidly as Time-to-Contact decreases

interactions.label: Forward Velocity (V_z)
interactions.teaches: Varying V_z scales the magnitude of the flow vectors proportionally, while their radial direction from the FOE remains unchanged

interactions.label: Sequential Cameras
interactions.teaches: Toggles between showing a single active camera and showing five sequential camera frames simultaneously to replicate the textbook's trajectory illustration

interactions.label: Minimalist Style
interactions.teaches: Switches to a minimalist style that hides flow vectors and grid details to match the clean aesthetic of the textbook figure

interactions.label: Multi-depth scene
interactions.teaches: Swapping from a flat wall to points of varying depths reveals that closer elements produce larger outward flow vectors due to the inverse scaling by depth Z

demo_steps.title: The Textbook Camera Path
demo_steps.narration: This visualizes the textbook diagram directly: five sequential cameras are aligned along the Z-axis. The red arrow highlights the camera's forward translation path, showing how perspective projection scales up as the camera moves closer to the target.

demo_steps.title: Detailed Flow Field Analysis
demo_steps.narration: Let's isolate a single camera and look at the actual motion field. Under forward translation, all 2D optical flow vectors point radially outward from a single point of zero motion — the Focus of Expansion (FOE), marked in yellow.

demo_steps.title: Approaching the Wall
demo_steps.narration: As we translate the camera forward from Z = -8 to Z = -3.5, the flow vectors dramatically lengthen. This occurs because the flow magnitude scales inversely with depth (V_z / Z); as depth Z approaches zero, expansion accelerates.

demo_steps.title: Depth Parallax during Forward Motion
demo_steps.narration: By switching to a multi-depth scene, we see how depth modulates this expansion. Spheres closer to the camera produce much longer flow vectors than spheres in the distant background, despite having the same lateral coordinates.

demo_steps.title: Effect of Velocity
demo_steps.narration: Increasing the forward velocity V_z scales up all flow vectors. While the direction of expansion remains locked to the optical axis, a higher speed means objects are expanding outwards at a much faster rate.

plan.camera_suggestion: Perspective view from slightly elevated, behind and to the left of the starting camera (e.g., eye: [-8, 5, -13], target: [0, 0, 0]) to clearly show the trajectory vector, the sequential cameras, and the 3D scene depth simultaneously.
plan.notes: When 'Sequential Cameras' is true, render 5 wireframe frustums spaced at Z = [-10, -8, -6, -4, -2]. When 'Minimalist Style' is active, render the scene with high-contrast, clean wireframes/silhouettes, and hide the optical flow vector arrows and background grid details to match the classic textbook aesthetic.

elements: 3D camera frustum representing the moving physical camera with lens center and projection plane
Forward translation path vector (long red arrow along the Z-axis of translation)
Target 3D scene: a grid-patterned wall representing the planar surface
Secondary target 3D scene: several floating colored spheres placed at varying depths (Z-coordinates) to showcase parallax
Focus of Expansion (FOE) marker (bright yellow target dot) in the center of the projection plane
2D optical flow vector arrows drawn on the camera projection plane, pointing radially outward from the FOE
Visual projection rays connecting 3D scene points through the camera center to the projection plane

interactions.label: Camera Position (Z)
interactions.teaches: Moving the camera closer to the scene demonstrates the spatial expansion of projected features as Time-to-Contact decreases

interactions.label: Forward Velocity (V_z)
interactions.teaches: Higher velocity increases the magnitude of all optical flow vectors proportionally, following the equation: flow = (V_z / Z) * radial_distance

interactions.label: Multi-depth scene
interactions.teaches: Swapping from a flat wall to a multi-depth cloud of points reveals that depth (Z) acts as a denominator: closer objects produce much larger flow vectors than distant objects at the same lateral position

interactions.label: Show Flow Vectors
interactions.teaches: Overlays the 2D optical flow field vectors on the image plane, showing the outward radial expansion pattern

demo_steps.title: The Setup: Forward Translation
demo_steps.narration: A camera is positioned on the Z-axis, pointing straight ahead at a target wall. Because the translation velocity V_z is active, a motion field is generated on the projection plane. Notice that all optical flow vectors point radially outward from a single point.

demo_steps.title: Focus of Expansion (FOE)
demo_steps.narration: Look closely at the yellow marker in the center of the image. This is the Focus of Expansion (FOE). Because the camera is moving straight along its optical axis, the point of impact has zero motion. All other points expand away from this origin.

demo_steps.title: Approaching the wall
demo_steps.narration: As we translate the camera forward (closer to the wall), watch how the optical flow vectors dramatically lengthen. As depth Z decreases, the term V_z / Z grows rapidly, accelerating the outward expansion rate until the moment of contact.

demo_steps.title: The effect of variable depth
demo_steps.narration: By switching to a multi-depth scene, we can observe the role of depth Z. Look at the flow vectors of the nearest spheres compared to those far in the background: even at identical lateral offsets, closer elements expand much faster due to the inverse scaling by depth.

demo_steps.title: Slowing down the flow
demo_steps.narration: If we reduce the camera's velocity V_z, the flow magnitudes instantly scale down. The structural directions of the vectors remain perfectly radial, but the rate of expansion drops, reflecting a longer Time-to-Contact.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Perspective view from slightly behind and to the left of the camera's starting position (e.g., eye: [-6, 4, -12], target: [0, 0, 0]), allowing the user to simultaneously see the 3D scene depth and the projected 2D vectors on the physical camera's image plane.
interactionPlan.notes: Render the camera's projection plane as a semi-transparent rectangle. Draw the 2D optical flow vectors directly on this plane in a highly contrasting color like neon green or bright cyan. Ensure the 3D projection lines constantly snap to the camera center as the 'cameraZ' slider updates.

attempts.html:
The Setup: Forward Translation
A camera is positioned on the Z-axis, pointing straight ahead at a target wall.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The original figure shows a horizontal sequence of five cameras simultaneously, whereas the generated figure shows a single camera in a 3D environment pointing at a wall.
The original figure has red arrows pointing from camera center to camera center in a sequence, whereas the generated figure has a single red velocity arrow.
The original figure has a minimalist style with no background grid wall, multi-depth spheres, or flow vectors, which are heavily featured in the generated version.
evaluation.notes: While the interactive demo is highly educational and explains the physics of forward translation extremely well, it lacks the sequential 5-camera layout shown in the original static figure.
action_items: Add a toggle or view mode to display the five sequential cameras in a row as shown in the original figure to directly mirror the textbook illustration.
Allow the user to see the progression of the camera center moving along the red arrow path through the five discrete steps simultaneously in 3D.
Provide a 'Minimalist/Textbook' visual style option that hides the grid wall and flow vectors to match the clean aesthetic of the source diagram.

feedback.rationale: The generator successfully built a highly interactive demo, but the core structure is incorrect because the plan opted for a single moving camera instead of representing the simultaneous five-camera sequence from the original diagram.
action_items: Add a toggle or view mode to display the five sequential cameras in a row as shown in the original figure to directly mirror the textbook illustration.
Allow the user to see the progression of the camera center moving along the red arrow path through the five discrete steps simultaneously in 3D.
Provide a 'Minimalist/Textbook' visual style option that hides the grid wall and flow vectors to match the clean aesthetic of the source diagram.
actionItems: Add a toggle or view mode to display the five sequential cameras in a row as shown in the original figure to directly mirror the textbook illustration.
Allow the user to see the progression of the camera center moving along the red arrow path through the five discrete steps simultaneously in 3D.
Provide a 'Minimalist/Textbook' visual style option that hides the grid wall and flow vectors to match the clean aesthetic of the source diagram.

attempts.html:
Five successive camera positions
The repeated camera symbols form the original left-to-right sequence. Each red arrow marks forward translation to the next pose.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The 6th red arrow pointing to the right, exiting from the 5th camera pose, is missing.
The original figure has zero text labels, whereas the generated code defaults to showing several labels ('pose 1', 'successive camera positions', etc.).
The camera frustum lines in the source are thinner, cleaner black lines compared to the thicker 3D bars used in the generated view.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive reproduction of the sequence with play controls and spacing sliders, but it is missing the final outbound arrow on the far right and defaults to showing several non-original text labels.
action_items: Add a 6th red arrow pointing to the right from the 5th camera to fully match the source figure's pattern.
Change the default state of 'Show explanatory labels' to unchecked so the user initially sees the clean, unlabeled original diagram.
Slightly reduce the thickness of the black lines (bars) representing the camera frustum to match the thin wireframe style of the source image.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual structure and interactivity are highly rated, so we only need to refine the implementation to fix the missing arrow, default label visibility, and line thicknesses.

evaluation.notes: Excellent reproduction of the textbook sequence with high-quality interactive features and highly customizable options.
action_items: The camera models and red arrows are extremely faithful to the source vector illustration
The spacing control slider works smoothly to alter the layout dynamically
Play/Pause sequence buttons and toggles for annotations/gray image-plane lines add rich value

feedback.rationale: The figure received perfect scores of 5 across all criteria with no discrepancies or failure modes, indicating that no further iteration is needed.
